condition.qualitative_description: Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or at least one severe week combined with four or more moderate weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Latest kharif seasonal rainfall is below 75% of the long-term mean annual precipitation, and the multi-year annual precipitation trend is declining, confirming a structural rainfall deficit rather than a single anomalous year.
Total surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct SWBs exist in the MWS, indicating both shrinking storage and an infrastructure gap.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA in the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.
Surface water body total area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.
Mean cropping intensity at or below 1.15 with no upward trend over the observation period confirms persistent single-season rainfed farming with no expansion of irrigated double-cropping.

No canal irrigation infrastructure intersects the micro-watershed AND fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been recorded, jointly confirming absence of both formal surface irrigation and community-level irrigation investment.
Forest-to-farm conversion area has increased over the observation period, indicating progressive encroachment of agricultural activity into forest land.

Village has a high Scheduled Tribe population (>30%) coinciding with measurable forest-to-farm conversion, indicating that forest rights disputes or displacement may be driving NTFP access decline for the most dependent communities.

Forest-to-farm conversion exceeds 100 ha over the record period in non-plains terrain, indicating substantial encroachment pressure in hilly or plateau landscapes where forest access routes are limited.
More than 50 hectares of cumulative deforestation recorded in the micro-watershed, representing a significant absolute loss of forest habitat.

Fewer than 5 cumulative MGNREGA soil and water conservation works recorded in the micro-watershed, indicating insufficient wage employment provision and low investment in productive assets during lean seasons.

Fewer than 5 cumulative MGNREGA soil and water conservation works in the micro-watershed, indicating insufficient public employment and watershed investment to buffer households against agricultural income shocks.

Village is more than 30 km from the nearest APMC and more than 20 km from the nearest dairy collection centre, indicating that small holders face high transaction costs and are unable to diversify into livestock income.

The linear trend in annual groundwater balance (P - ET - Runoff) is declining at more than 2 mm/year and the multi-year mean is negative, confirming chronic depletion exceeding recharge in the arid zone.

Alluvial aquifer confirmed with mean annual precipitation below 450 mm, indicating that despite high storage capacity, recharge is severely limited by aridity — a combination that amplifies depletion risk from irrigation extraction.
Both the linear trend and the multi-year mean of annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) are negative, indicating chronic depletion rather than a single drought year anomaly.

Dominant alluvial aquifer lithology (>50% of MWS area) in a coastal plain setting confirms high recharge potential but also high extraction vulnerability due to ease of borewell installation and intensive irrigation demand.
Both the linear trend and the multi-year mean of annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) are negative, indicating chronic depletion rather than a single drought year anomaly.
Dominant aquifer class is crystalline basement with less than 20% alluvial lithology, confirming low recharge capacity and high vulnerability to depletion.
Aquifer classified as volcanic or crystalline basement, indicating low storativity and limited lateral recharge typical of island geology in Andaman & Nicobar and Lakshadweep.

Linear trend in annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) is negative and mean annual recharge surplus is below 20 mm, indicating that despite high rainfall, effective recharge to the aquifer is declining or marginal.
Dominant alluvial aquifer with more than 40% of the micro-watershed area underlain by alluvial lithology, confirming the aquifer type context and its recharge-discharge dynamics.
Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or co-occurrence of at least one severe week with four or more moderate weeks, indicates sustained crop-season water deficit.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

condition.expression: mean_kharif_precipitation < 0.75 * mean_annual_precipitation_mm or mean_annual_precipitation_mm < 700 or trend_annual_precipitation_mm < -5
condition.qualitative_description: Kharif seasonal rainfall below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or mean annual precipitation below 700 mm, or a declining trend exceeding 5 mm/year confirms structural precipitation deficit underpinning drought risk.

SPI at or below -1.0 (moderate meteorological drought), MAI below 0.5 (soil moisture inadequacy), and VCI below 35 (significant vegetation stress) together confirm concurrent meteorological and agricultural drought per India Drought Manual classification.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Mean annual precipitation below 1000 mm (lower bound of AER-18 normal range of 900–1400 mm) or current kharif seasonal rainfall below 65% of the long-term kharif mean indicates a below-normal rainfall year consistent with meteorological drought.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal precipitation below 75% of the long-run kharif normal, or mean annual precipitation below 1000 mm with a declining trend exceeding 10 mm/year, confirms a rainfall deficit context consistent with recurrent drought.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate weeks, indicates persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture stress during critical crop growth stages.

Mean annual precipitation below 950 mm, or kharif seasonal precipitation below 700 mm in the latest year, or a declining trend exceeding 10 mm/year with mean below 1100 mm, indicates structural rainfall deficit in this sub-humid zone.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or at least one severe week combined with four or more moderate drought weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% of historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal rainfall below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or a declining multi-year precipitation trend below 900 mm/year, confirms meteorological drought as the primary driver of water scarcity.

Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks in the most recent kharif season, indicate persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss in rice and other kharif crops.

Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the most recent kharif season indicate prolonged intra-seasonal moisture gaps that disrupt critical crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal rainfall in the most recent year below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or mean annual precipitation below 1200 mm, indicates a below-normal rainfall year consistent with meteorological drought in AER-13.

Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks, in the most recent kharif season indicate a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress in an otherwise humid zone.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the most recent kharif season indicate a prolonged moisture deficit disrupting crop growth stages.

Monsoon onset is later than the earliest year on record AND kharif seasonal precipitation in the most recent year is below 80% of the multi-year mean, jointly indicating a delayed and deficient monsoon.
Three or more severe drought weeks, or five or more moderate drought weeks in the most recent kharif season, indicate persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss.

A declining multi-year precipitation trend (>5 mm/year reduction) combined with a below-normal kharif season rainfall (less than 75% of expected kharif share) confirms a structural rainfall deficit driving drought.
Two or more severe drought weeks, or four or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate meaningful crop-season water deficit even in a perhumid zone.
Three or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture stress periods that disrupt crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal precipitation below 75% of the long-run annual mean, or a declining precipitation trend exceeding 20mm/year combined with mean annual rainfall below 1800mm, signals structural drought risk in this perhumid zone.
Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate significant meteorological drought stress even in a normally humid island ecoregion.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate a prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Three or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate a prolonged intra-seasonal break that disrupts crop water supply.

Kharif seasonal precipitation in the latest year below 75% of the long-run kharif normal, or a declining trend in annual precipitation exceeding 20 mm/year, confirms a structural rainfall deficit underpinning drought.

Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged rainfall gaps that disrupt critical crop growth stages.

Long-run mean annual precipitation below 600 mm combined with a kharif season receiving less than 75% of the long-run annual mean confirms a below-normal monsoon year in an already moisture-limited zone.

A declining trend in annual precipitation exceeding 30 mm/year, or a kharif seasonal total below 75% of the long-term mean annual precipitation, confirms a below-normal rainfall year driving drought stress.
